# Imitation Learning {.section-divider}

## Minimal Setup

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
- We observe expert demonstrations of the task.
- Each demonstration provides observations and actions.
- The goal is to learn a policy that reproduces expert-like behavior.

$$
\mathcal{D}
=
\left\{
\tau_i
\right\}_{i=1}^{M},
\qquad
\tau_i =
\left[
(o_1,a_1),\ldots,(o_T,a_T)
\right]
$$
:::

::: {.equation-card}
$$
\pi_\theta(a_t \mid o_t)
$$

where $o_t$ is the robot observation and $a_t$ is the expert action.
:::
:::


::: {.notes}
The first thing I want to make precise is the object we are learning. In imitation learning, we assume that someone, or some system, can demonstrate the task. From those demonstrations we record trajectories: observations and actions over time.

The observation $o_t$ may be an image, joint positions, end-effector pose, force-torque measurements, tactile signals, language instructions, or a combination of these. The action $a_t$ may be a joint velocity, an end-effector displacement, a target pose, a gripper command, or a lower-level control parameter.

So the policy $\pi_\theta$ is the learned mapping from what the robot senses to what the expert would do. I write it probabilistically, $\pi_\theta(a_t \mid o_t)$, because later we will care about distributions over actions, not only one deterministic prediction.

$\mathcal{D}$ is the dataset. Each $\tau_i$ is one demonstration trajectory. $M$ is the number of demonstrations, while $T$ is the length of one trajectory in timesteps. The pair $(o_t,a_t)$ means "observation at time $t$" and "expert action at the same time." $\theta$ denotes all trainable parameters of the policy network: weights and biases. $\pi_\theta(a_t \mid o_t)$ means a conditional distribution over actions, not just a function value. In a deterministic policy this distribution collapses to one preferred action; in a stochastic or generative policy it can represent several plausible actions.

One useful warning here is that $o_t$ is not necessarily the true state of the world. In robotics, the true state may include hidden contact conditions, object pose uncertainty, friction, or deformation. The policy only receives the information we choose to measure and represent.
:::

---

## Behavioral Cloning

::: {.compact}
- Treat expert actions as supervised learning targets.
- Fit the policy on demonstration samples; simple and scalable — used since ALVINN, the first end-to-end neural driving policy [@pomerleau1988alvinn].
:::

$$
\mathcal{L}_{BC}(\theta)
=
\mathbb{E}_{(o_t,a_t)\sim\mathcal{D}}
\left[
\lVert \pi_\theta(o_t) - a_t \rVert^2
\right]
$$

![](assets/figures/behavioral_cloning_pipeline.svg){width=82% fig-align="center"}

::: {.notes}
Behavioral cloning is the most direct imitation learning method. We take the dataset of expert observation-action pairs and train a model by supervised learning. In the simplest continuous-action case, we minimize a squared error between the predicted action and the demonstrated action.

In the loss, $\mathcal{L}_{BC}(\theta)$ is the training objective for the policy parameters. The expectation $\mathbb{E}_{(o_t,a_t)\sim\mathcal{D}}$ means "average this error over samples drawn from the demonstration dataset." $\pi_\theta(o_t)$ is the policy's predicted action for observation $o_t$. The term $\lVert \pi_\theta(o_t)-a_t\rVert^2$ is the squared Euclidean distance between the predicted action vector and the expert action vector. If the action has six dimensions, the loss sums the squared error over those six dimensions. That is why behavioral cloning is just supervised regression when actions are continuous.

Behavioral cloning is attractive because it uses the same machinery as standard deep learning. We can train with minibatches, use images and proprioception as inputs, scale to large datasets, and evaluate prediction error on held-out demonstrations.

But we should be careful about what the loss means. A low behavioral cloning loss tells us that the policy predicts expert actions well on states that look like the training data. It does not automatically tell us that the policy will recover well from its own mistakes after it is deployed in closed loop.

Also, the squared loss assumes that the average of plausible expert actions is itself a good action. That assumption is often false in manipulation. If there are two valid ways to approach an object, the mean of those two actions may point toward neither valid strategy.
:::

---

## Why Not Only Reinforcement Learning?

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
- Reward design is difficult for contact-rich manipulation.
- Exploration can be unsafe, slow, or damaging on hardware.
- Resetting physical systems is expensive.
- Demonstrations provide structure before trial-and-error optimization.
:::

::: {.warning}
Reinforcement learning is powerful, but on real robots the data collection loop is constrained by time, safety, wear, and reset logistics.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
A natural question is: why not solve the whole problem with reinforcement learning? In principle, reinforcement learning is a very general framework. The robot tries actions, receives rewards, and improves its policy.

The difficulty is that real robot learning has a very expensive interaction loop. Each trial takes physical time. Failed exploration can damage the robot, the object, or the environment. Many manipulation tasks require manual resets. And for contact-rich tasks, designing a reward that captures both task success and safe physical interaction is not trivial.

Imitation learning changes the starting point. Instead of beginning with random exploration, we start from demonstrations that already contain useful structure: where to move, when to slow down, how to approach contact, and how to recover from small errors.

This does not mean imitation learning and reinforcement learning are mutually exclusive. A common strategy is to use demonstrations to initialize a policy and then improve it with interaction. For this lecture, imitation learning is important because Diffusion Policy is trained from demonstrations and must later be executed as a real-time robot policy.
:::

---

## Covariate Shift

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
- During training, states come from the **expert** policy.
- During deployment, states come from the **learned** policy — its own errors push it into unseen states.
- No demonstrations show how to *recover*, so the next action is even less reliable [@ross2010efficient].

$$
\underbrace{o_t \sim d^{\pi_E}}_{\text{training}}
\;\neq\;
\underbrace{o_t \sim d^{\pi_\theta}}_{\text{execution}}
$$
:::

::: {}
![](assets/figures/covariate_shift.svg){width=100%}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Covariate shift is the classic failure mode of behavioral cloning. During training, the data distribution is generated by the expert policy. During deployment, the state distribution is generated by the learned policy. These two distributions may be different.

In the notation, $d^{\pi_E}$ is the distribution of observations visited when the expert policy $\pi_E$ controls the system. $d^{\pi_\theta}$ is the distribution visited when the learned policy controls the system. The symbol $\neq$ is the whole problem: the supervised learner is trained on one distribution but evaluated on another. This is not just a statistical nuisance; the policy changes the future data distribution through its own actions.

Imagine an expert trajectory that stays centered during insertion. The learned policy makes a small lateral error. After that error, the robot observes a state that may not be well represented in the dataset. The next action is then less reliable, which can create an even larger error. This is how small prediction mistakes compound over time.

This is especially important in contact-rich manipulation because the environment can amplify small pose errors into large forces or contact switches. A policy that looks good on validation data may still fail when it drifts away from the expert manifold.

There are several ways to address this, such as collecting corrective demonstrations, using dataset aggregation, adding noise and recovery examples, or using closed-loop policy architectures. For today, the important point is that imitation learning is not only a supervised learning problem; it is supervised learning embedded inside a closed-loop dynamical system.
:::

---

## Why Errors Compound

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
- On **expert states**, each error is bounded by $\epsilon$ → total error grows **linearly**, $\sim \epsilon\,T$.
- In **closed loop**, an error shifts the next state, so later errors stack on top → total error grows **quadratically**, $\sim \epsilon\,T^2$.
- Longer horizons make behavioral cloning disproportionately fragile [@ross2010efficient; @bacciu_il_lecture].
:::

![](assets/figures/error_compounding.svg){width=100%}
:::

::: {.equation-card}
$$
\underbrace{\mathbb{E}[E] \lesssim \epsilon\,T}_{\text{i.i.d. / supervised}}
\qquad\text{vs.}\qquad
\underbrace{\mathbb{E}[E] \lesssim \epsilon\,\big(T + (T{-}1) + \cdots\big) \propto \epsilon\,T^2}_{\text{behavioral cloning, closed loop}}
$$
:::

::: {.notes}
We can make the covariate-shift argument quantitative. Suppose the policy makes a per-step mistake with probability at most epsilon. If at every step the agent were placed back on a state from the expert distribution — the i.i.d. supervised-learning assumption — the expected number of mistakes over a horizon of T steps would simply be of order epsilon times T: errors are independent in time.

The quantity $\mathbb{E}[E]$ means expected total error or expected number of bad decisions over the rollout. $\epsilon$ is the single-step error rate under the training distribution. $T$ is the task horizon. The symbol $\lesssim$ means "bounded up to constants" rather than exact equality. The first expression, $\epsilon T$, is what supervised learning intuition suggests: more timesteps means proportionally more chances to make a mistake. The second expression, $\epsilon T^2$, says that in closed loop the cost of an early mistake can persist for many future steps.

But a robot runs in closed loop. A mistake at step t changes the state at t+1, which is now off the expert manifold, where the policy is even more likely to err. So a single early mistake can corrupt the entire remainder of the trajectory. Summing T + (T-1) + ... gives an expected error that scales like epsilon times T squared — quadratic in the horizon. This is the classical reduction analysis of Ross and Bagnell.

The practical message is behavioral cloning quality degrades faster than supervised validation loss would suggest, and the longer the task, the worse the gap. This is exactly why the field cares about remedies like DAgger, action chunking, and closed-loop policies.
:::

---

## Addressing Covariate Shift: DAgger

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
**Key idea:** if we cannot make the policy's state distribution match the expert's, change the *data* to match the policy's [@ross2011dagger].

- Roll out the current policy; record the states it actually visits.
- Ask the expert for the correct action on **those** states.
- Aggregate into the dataset and retrain — repeat.

Training and deployment then see the same state distribution.
:::

![](assets/figures/dagger_loop.svg){width=100%}
:::

::: {.notes}
DAgger — Dataset Aggregation — is the canonical remedy for covariate shift. The insight is a change of perspective: instead of hoping the learned policy reproduces the expert's state distribution, we deliberately collect labels on the states the learned policy actually reaches.

The loop is: train the policy on the current dataset; roll it out to gather the states it visits; ask the expert to provide the correct action for each of those states; aggregate the new labelled states into the dataset; and repeat. Over iterations the dataset comes to cover the policy's own induced distribution, so the train/deploy mismatch shrinks.

It is worth being honest about the costs. DAgger runs a partially trained — possibly unsafe — policy on the system; it needs repeated access to an expert; and the expert is asked to label states it never actually experienced, which can be unnatural. On real contact-rich robots these costs are significant, which is part of why we will instead lean on expressive closed-loop policies. This is the idea in Ross, Gordon, and Bagnell, 2011.
:::

---

## Multimodal Actions

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
For the same observation, several expert actions can be valid — steer left **or** right around an obstacle, grasp from either side, push or pull.

Mean-squared regression collapses the modes to their **average**. If $a^{(1)} \neq a^{(2)}$ are both valid,

$$
\bar{a} = \tfrac{1}{2}\big(a^{(1)} + a^{(2)}\big)
$$

can be physically meaningless — steering straight into the obstacle.
:::

![](assets/figures/multimodal_averaging.svg){width=100%}
:::

::: {.notes}
Many manipulation tasks are multimodal. From the same observation, the expert may have several valid choices. A robot can grasp an object from the left or from the right. It can push first and then grasp, or grasp directly. During contact, it may continue applying gentle pressure or retreat and re-approach.

In the formula, $a^{(1)}$ and $a^{(2)}$ are two different valid expert actions for the same observation. $\bar{a}$ is their arithmetic mean. Mean-squared regression rewards predicting $\bar{a}$ because it minimizes average squared distance to both examples. The problem is semantic, not arithmetic: in continuous robot action space the midpoint between two good strategies can be a bad strategy. A multimodal policy should represent "left grasp" and "right grasp" as separate modes rather than collapsing them.

This is a problem for deterministic regression with a mean-squared error loss. If the dataset contains multiple valid modes, the model is encouraged to predict an average action. But the average of two valid strategies is not necessarily a valid strategy.

This is one of the key motivations for distributional policies. Instead of predicting only one action, we want the policy to represent a distribution of plausible expert-like actions. The distribution should still be conditioned on the observation and task. Multimodal does not mean arbitrary; it means there may be several structured, valid choices.

Diffusion models connect directly to diffusion models, because diffusion gives us a way to model complex high-dimensional distributions over actions or action trajectories.
:::

---

## Transition to Generative Policies

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
- Deterministic policies predict one action.
- Generative policies model a conditional action distribution.
- Classical fixes — Gaussian mixtures $\textstyle\sum_i w_i\,\mathcal{N}(\mu_i,\Sigma_i)$ [@bishop1994mdn], autoregressive discretization — need a guessed number of modes or careful tuning.
- For robotics, we often want distributions over action *sequences*, not only one-step actions.

$$
A_{t:t+H}
\sim
p_\theta(A_{t:t+H} \mid o_t)
$$
:::

::: {.takeaway}
Diffusion Policy uses a generative model to sample coherent action trajectories conditioned on robot observations.
:::
:::


::: {.notes}
We are now ready to transition from classical behavioral cloning to generative policies. The key shift is that the policy is no longer just a deterministic function that returns one best action. Instead, it represents a conditional distribution over possible actions.

In robot manipulation, it is often even more useful to generate a short horizon of future actions, $A_{t:t+H}$, rather than one command at a time. A sequence can encode timing, smoothness, and commitment to one mode of behavior. For example, choosing a left-side grasp is not just one action; it is a coherent short trajectory.

In the notation, $A_{t:t+H}$ is the action sequence from the current time $t$ to a future horizon $t+H$. The distribution $p_\theta(A_{t:t+H}\mid o_t)$ says: given the current observation, assign probability to whole future action chunks. This is different from predicting $a_t$ alone. A chunk can be internally consistent: if the first action starts moving left around an object, the following actions can continue that left-side strategy.

The Gaussian-mixture expression $\sum_i w_i\,\mathcal{N}(\mu_i,\Sigma_i)$ is a classical way to represent multiple modes. Each Gaussian component has a mean $\mu_i$, covariance $\Sigma_i$, and mixture weight $w_i$. The weights are nonnegative and sum to one. This can model several possible actions, but we often have to choose the number of components in advance, and training can be delicate in high-dimensional sequence spaces. Autoregressive discretization is another alternative: predict one discrete action token at a time. That can work, but it introduces discretization choices and can be awkward for smooth continuous robot actions.

Diffusion Policy follows this idea. It uses a diffusion model as the generative mechanism for action trajectories conditioned on observations. This gives the model enough expressiveness to represent multimodal demonstrations and high-dimensional continuous actions.

Generation alone does not solve control: generation alone does not solve control. A sampled trajectory still has to be executed on a real robot with latency, stability, force limits, and safety constraints. That is why the rest of the lecture will move from diffusion models to efficient inference and then to compliant control.
:::
